claim: Hand B's section-sensitivity on TTR and chedy-frequency (from H-BV-HAND-B-SECTION-INDEPENDENCE-01) is driven primarily by the biological section (19 folios). If biological-exclusion removes the effect, Hand B content-coupling is restricted to biological visual content; if the effect persists, section-coupling is broader. Cross-hand herbal comparison (Hand A herbal vs Hand B herbal) tests whether the within-same-section difference reflects scribal convention or content.
evidence_for: H-BV-HAND-B-SECTION-INDEPENDENCE-01 REFUTED established that Hand B's TTR varies by section (F=61, p<0.0001) and chedy frequency varies by section (F=4.38, p<0.0001). The pattern is real; the question is what drives it.

The biological section is visually distinctive in the Voynich: naked female figures, bathing scenes, interconnected tubes, fluid-like structures. This is the section with the most unambiguously narrative illustration content. If the text encodes ANY content, biological would be the most likely section to show distinct text statistics.

Hand A has ZERO biological folios at the >=30-token threshold; Hand B has 19 biological folios. Biological is a Hand-B-exclusive content type within the Hand A/B comparison frame. If biological drives Hand B's section effect, the inter-hand asymmetry makes immediate sense: Hand A never writes about biological content.
Pairwise comparisons will directly identify which section pairs differ. Welch t-test on means is appropriate for unequal-variance groups.
evidence_against: Hand B's section distribution is 32 herbal / 23 recipes / 19 biological / 5 text-only / 3 cosmological. Biological is 23% of Hand B folios with >=30 tokens; an effect driven 'primarily' by biological could still leave detectable effects from the other 77%. Partial driver rather than sole driver is a plausible outcome.
Welch t-tests require adequate sample sizes per group. text-only (5) and cosmological (3) are below ideal; their pairwise results will have high variance.

Cross-hand herbal comparison depends on the TTR/chedy metrics being defined comparably across hands. Hand A doesn't have chedy as a frequent token — so cross-hand chedy comparison compares Hand A's essentially-zero chedy rate to Hand B's nonzero rate, which is descriptive, not diagnostic.

If the biological-exclusion test shows the effect PERSISTS even without biological, we've shown broader section-coupling but without identifying specific other contributors. Additional follow-up would be needed.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: Hand B folios with >=30 tokens from voynich-corpus.json. Same filtering as H-BV-HAND-B-SECTION-INDEPENDENCE-01.

  SECTIONS WITH n>=3 folios: herbal (32), recipes (23), biological (19), text-only (5), cosmological (3).

  METRICS: TTR (types/tokens), chedy_freq (count of 'chedy' / total tokens).

  SUB-TEST 1 — PAIRWISE SECTION COMPARISONS:
    For each unordered pair of sections with both having n>=3 folios (10 pairs total):
      For each metric (TTR, chedy_freq):
        Welch's t-test on folio-level metric values between the two sections.
        Report t-statistic and two-tailed p-value.
    Apply Bonferroni correction for 10 pairs x 2 metrics = 20 tests: corrected p < 0.0025.
    Identify which pairs show significant differences under Bonferroni correction.

  SUB-TEST 2 — BIOLOGICAL-EXCLUSION:
    Re-run H-BV-HAND-B-SECTION-INDEPENDENCE-01's C1 (Section x TTR) and C4 (Section x chedy_freq) ANOVAs with biological folios EXCLUDED.
    Sections in the exclusion run: herbal (32), recipes (23), text-only (5), cosmological (3). n_total = 63.
    Shuffle-null 1000 samples. Record observed F and p-value.
    REPORT: F and p for each metric. Compare to original C1 (F=61, p<0.0001) and C4 (F=4.38, p<0.0001).
    Interpretation:
      If new p < 0.01 on either: biological is NOT sole driver — other sections contribute.
      If new p >= 0.10 on both: biological IS sole driver — effect vanishes without it.
      Intermediate: partial driver.

  SUB-TEST 3 — SINGLE-SECTION-VS-REST:
    For each Hand B section with n>=3 folios:
      For each metric:
        Welch's t-test: this section's folio values vs all OTHER sections' folio values combined.
        Report t-statistic, p-value, and effect size (Cohen's d).
    Identify which section contributes largest effect to each metric.

  SUB-TEST 4 — CROSS-HAND HERBAL COMPARISON:
    Compare Hand A herbal folios to Hand B herbal folios on TTR and (separately) marker_freq.
    Hand A marker = daiin (Hand A's most frequent token); Hand B marker = chedy.
    This is a within-section, across-hand comparison — does the same content section produce statistically different text across scribes/hands?
    Welch's t-test for each metric.
    Also compare MEAN TOKEN LENGTH (comparable across hands using same definition).
    Interpretation: if herbal-A and herbal-B differ significantly on TTR or length, scribal convention matters within-section. If null, the within-section statistics are hand-invariant.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    BIOLOGICAL_SOLE_DRIVER: Sub-test 2 biological-exclusion yields p >= 0.10 on BOTH C1 and C4. Effect vanishes without biological.
    BIOLOGICAL_DOMINANT: Sub-test 2 exclusion yields p in [0.01, 0.10) on either C1 or C4 (effect weakens substantially). AND Sub-test 3 shows biological's single-vs-rest effect is the largest for both metrics.
    DISTRIBUTED_EFFECT: Sub-test 2 exclusion yields p < 0.01 on EITHER C1 or C4. Other sections contribute alongside biological.
    CROSS_HAND_SCRIBAL_OVERLAY: if Sub-test 4 shows herbal-A vs herbal-B differ significantly (p < 0.01) on TTR or mean length, scribal convention is a confounder; even the same content type produces different statistics per hand.

  REPORTING: all four sub-test results plus aggregate verdict per decision rule.

  WORKED EXAMPLE (Sub-test 1 pair biological vs herbal-B):
    Biological TTR values: [ttr_f75r, ttr_f75v, ...] for 19 folios.
    Herbal-B TTR values: [ttr_f...] for 32 folios.
    Welch t-test: t = (mean_bio - mean_herbal) / sqrt(s_bio^2/n_bio + s_herbal^2/n_herbal).
    Approximate p from Student's t distribution (two-tailed).
test_metric: 4 sub-tests with per-subtest verdicts. Aggregate classification per decision rule.
test_threshold: Decision rule bins: BIOLOGICAL_SOLE_DRIVER, BIOLOGICAL_DOMINANT, DISTRIBUTED_EFFECT, CROSS_HAND_SCRIBAL_OVERLAY (separable, can combine with other verdicts).

S1_pairwise_significant_at_bonferroni.note: Large effect — recipes LOWER TTR than herbal
Recipes has much LESS chedy than biological
Enormous effect — herbal HIGH TTR (0.83), biological LOW TTR (0.56)

TTR_largest_effect.note: Herbal has MUCH higher TTR than non-herbal. This is the single strongest driver of the TTR section effect.

TTR_second_largest.note: Biological has LOW TTR. Pulls the other direction from herbal.

marker_freq_largest_effect.note: Biological has 70% HIGHER chedy frequency than other sections. Sole driver of chedy section effect.

marker_freq_second.note: Cosmological low chedy, but only n=3, high variance

TTR.verdict: IDENTICAL — no scribal overlay on TTR within herbal

mean_length.verdict: STATISTICALLY INDISTINGUISHABLE — no scribal overlay on length

marker_freq.note: Markers differ by definition (daiin vs chedy), so this is descriptive not diagnostic. Hand A uses daiin ~2.7x more often than Hand B uses chedy in herbal. Reflects different most-common-tokens per hand, not scribal overlay.

metric_specific_verdicts.TTR: DISTRIBUTED_EFFECT — herbal drives positively, recipes+biological drive negatively. Biological-exclusion leaves F=37.66 p<0.0001 — effect clearly persists.
metric_specific_verdicts.chedy_freq: BIOLOGICAL_SOLE_DRIVER — without biological the effect completely vanishes (p=0.85). Biological has 70% more chedy than other sections.

verdict_note: DISTRIBUTED_EFFECT primary verdict, but with METRIC-SPECIFIC drivers. TTR section effect persists without biological (F=37.66, p<0.0001 after exclusion) — driven by herbal (d=+3.05, huge positive) and recipes (d=-0.64) with biological contributing a secondary negative pull (d=-1.65). Chedy section effect is BIOLOGICAL_SOLE_DRIVER (without biological, effect vanishes at p=0.85). Biological has 70% higher chedy frequency than other sections. No scribal overlay detected on herbal-A vs herbal-B (TTR identical at 0.83, mean length within 0.08 chars). The picture: Hand B's TTR varies because different sections have genuinely different vocabulary-diversity patterns (herbal diverse, recipes compact), while chedy specifically concentrates in biological content.
findings_for_followup: CRITICAL FOR v2: two distinct section effects with different drivers. TTR is a broad section-vocabulary-diversity signal driven primarily by herbal (high TTR) vs recipes/biological (low TTR). Chedy frequency is a narrow biological-specific marker. These require different v2 interpretations.

TTR PATTERN (distributed): herbal folios have MUCH more vocabulary diversity (0.83) than recipes (0.63) or biological (0.56) folios. Recipes are highly repetitive text; biological is also repetitive; herbal has more unique tokens per folio. This is consistent with recipes containing formulaic or list-like content, biological having restricted vocabulary around its topic, herbal each plant getting its own distinct nomenclature.

CHEDY PATTERN (biological-sole): chedy specifically concentrates in biological folios at 70% higher rate than elsewhere. Biological is the section with distinctive visual content (naked figures, bathing scenes, tube/fluid structures). The chedy concentration suggests chedy functions as a biological-context marker. This is the FIRST CONCRETE EVIDENCE in Brain-V's arc that a specific Voynichese token correlates with a specific content type.

NO SCRIBAL OVERLAY: Hand A herbal and Hand B herbal produce statistically identical TTR (0.83 vs 0.83, p=0.96) and indistinguishable mean length (4.99 vs 5.07, p=0.34). The same content section yields the same statistics across hands. Whatever distinguishes Hand A from Hand B, it is NOT a hand-specific way of encoding herbal content.

RECONCILING HAND A vs HAND B: Hand A section distribution is 95 herbal + 16 pharma + 2 recipes + 1 text-only. The 'section-independence' finding in H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 was largely driven by Hand A being 86% herbal — effectively a single-section sample. Hand B's 32 herbal + 23 recipes + 19 biological + 5 text-only + 3 cosmo distribution has enough non-herbal content for section effects to emerge. Hand A's apparent section-independence may be a sampling artefact: Hand A just doesn't have enough non-herbal content for sections to differ visibly.

v2 NARRATIVE UPDATE: the 'section-independent overlay' interpretation of Hand A (from EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01) must be qualified — Hand A's section-independence is partly a consequence of Hand A being dominantly herbal. Hand B, with its richer section composition, reveals genuine section-content coupling. The combined evidence is consistent with: 'the text does respond to section content; Hand A happened to be sampled from predominantly one section so inter-section contrasts were unavailable.'

CHEDY AS BIOLOGICAL MARKER: this is a concrete follow-up candidate. Test whether OTHER specific Hand B tokens (shedy, qokedy, qokain) show section-specific concentrations, and whether specific herbal-distinctive or recipes-distinctive tokens exist. If each section has its own set of concentrated markers, the 'text responds to content' reading is substantially strengthened.

H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 AMENDMENT NEEDED: the section-independence claim for Hand A should be amended with 'this finding is likely a consequence of Hand A's 86%-herbal sampling; Hand B with richer section composition shows genuine section effects on TTR and on specific-marker frequencies'.

METHODOLOGY LESSON 10 CANDIDATE: when comparing two corpora for 'same effect present' vs 'different effect present', check whether sample composition is comparable. A corpus sampled from one condition cannot show condition-differences regardless of underlying structure. EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01's Hand A result may have been under-powered for the section question because of composition.
reasoning: H-BV-HAND-B-SECTION-INDEPENDENCE-01 REFUTED showed Hand B is section-sensitive unlike Hand A. Two candidate explanations: (a) biological section alone drives the effect (consistent with biological being Hand B's unique visually-distinctive content); (b) section-coupling is distributed across multiple sections. This hypothesis distinguishes them via biological-exclusion and pairwise comparisons. Additionally tests cross-hand herbal — if herbal-A and herbal-B differ statistically on the same metrics that distinguish sections within Hand B, scribal convention is a confounder that complicates the content interpretation.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed diagnostic follow-up to H-BV-HAND-B-SECTION-INDEPENDENCE-01 REFUTED. Sharpens the interpretation of Hand B's section sensitivity.
